topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the lecture hall hummed with a monotonous, irritating buzz. Elias stared at the worn spine of his philosophy textbook, the paper feeling suddenly brittle beneath his fingertips. Every word on the page seemed to mock the uncertainty churning in his gut. He traced the grain of the wooden desk with his index finger, the movement slow, deliberate. Outside, the autumn rain lashed against the windowpane, mirroring the relentless pressure building behind his sternum. He hadn't slept properly in days, the silence of his cramped dorm room amplifying the frantic ticking of the clock on his bedside table. When the official notice finally arrived—a stark, impersonal letter slipped under his door—he didn't move immediately. He just sat, the air thick and heavy around him, his jaw tight, his gaze fixed on a distant point beyond the peeling paint of the hallway.

[story 2]

"It's just paperwork," Sarah mumbled, the words tasting like ash in her mouth. She picked at a loose thread on her worn sweater, avoiding eye contact with the administration representative who spoke in clipped, professional tones. Internally, Liam felt a deep, cold settling in his chest, a leaden weight that made every breath shallow. He leaned against the cold brick of the campus quad, watching students laugh and rush past, their energy vibrant and careless compared to his own inertia. He pulled his hoodie tighter around himself, the fabric offering little comfort against the chill that seemed to emanate from within. He didn't speak, just let the silence stretch between them, a vast, empty space where hope used to reside.

[story 3]

The campus library smelled perpetually of old paper and dust, a scent that now seemed suffocatingly heavy. Marcus sat hunched over a stack of discarded syllabi, his pen hovering uselessly over the blank page. He kept rubbing the bridge of his nose, a repetitive, almost frantic gesture. The realization that the entire structure he had invested so much of himself into was dissolving around him was a physical ache, a dull throb behind his eyes. He let out a slow, ragged exhale, the sound barely audible above the rustle of turning pages. He looked up briefly, meeting the empty expanse of the high ceiling, a profound sense of defeat washing over him, leaving him rooted to the spot, utterly still.
